coredata.dc:title: A photostable fluorescent probe for long-time imagining of lysosome in cell and nematode
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Changjian
Wang, Yueqi
Huang, Saipeng
Zhang, Xiaojie
Kang, Xiaoxuan
Sun, Yanchi
Hu, Ziyuan
Han, Lu
Du, Libo
Liu, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Lysosome fluorescent imaging has been widely used in the field of biological staining and diagnostics, which plays a key role in understanding intracellular metabolism and various physiological processes. However, for most currently used small-molecule lysotrackers, the photostability is often unsatisfactory when used for long-term and real-time visualization of lysosomal dynamics. Herein, we reported a new lysosome-targetable photostable fluorescent probe (i.e. MPL-NPA), and results showed that MPL-NAP possesses superior photostability, appreciable tolerance to pH change, low cytotoxicity and high lysosome targeting ability. These findings confirm that MPL-NAP is a well-suited imaging agent for targeting lysosome and enables long-term and real-time monitor of lysosome morphological changes under physiological processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lysosome imaging
Photostability
Long-term imagining
1,8-naphthalimides
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Long-time monitoring the dynamic variation of lysosome in cell apoptosis
Long-time monitoring the dynamic variation of lysosome in cell apoptosis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence characteristic of MPL-NPA: (a) the UV-absorption; (b) fluorescence excitation and emission spectroscopy; and (c) fluorescence intensity of MPL-NPA in Britton–Robinson buffer solution with different pH values.

The subcellular fluorescence signals generated by MPL-NPA. H9C2 cells were utilized to co-incubate with MPL-NPA and representative organelle trackers: (a) LysoTracker Red DND-99 for lysosomes, (b) Hoechst 33324 for nucleus, (c) MitoTracker® Deep Red FM for mitochondria, (d) Cytoplasm Tracker CMAC for cytoplasm.
The Multicellular imaging fluorescence signals of MPL-NPA. All cells were co-treated with MPL-NPA and LysoTracker Red DND-99 for 15 min.

(a) Confocal images from MPL-NAP on channel 1. (b) Confocal images from LysoTracker Red DND-99 on channel 2. (c) Bright-field images. (d) Merged images. (e) Intensity profile of ROIs across RAW264.7 cells. (f) Correlation plot of MPL-NAP and LysoTracker Red DND-99 intensities.

Photostability of MPL-NAP. Confocal microscopy images of H9C2 cells co-stained with MPL-NAP (a), LysoTracker Red DND-99 (b) with an increasing number of scans (1–30 scans). (c) Merged image. (d) Fluorescence intensity decay of MPL-NAP compared with LysoTracker Red DND-99 and LysoTracker Green DND-26. F0 is the max fluorescence intensity before the scan. F is the fluorescence intensity after an increasing number of scans.
Cytotoxicity of MPL-NPA on three cell lines determined by MTT assay.
Long-time and live-cells confocal images of H9C2 cells stained with 0.5 μM MPL-NAP and stimulated using 250 μM H2O2: (a) 0 s, (b) 5 min, (c) 10 min, (d) 15 min.

Confocal fluorescence images of Caenorhabditis elegans stained with (a, e) MPL-NPA and (b, f) LysoTracker Red DND-99, (c, g) bright-field images, (d, h) Merged image of a, b, c and e, f, g (a-d 40 ×magnified images; e-f 100 ×magnified images.).

head.ce:title: A photostable fluorescent probe for long-time imagining of lysosome in cell and nematode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Structural Chemistry of Unstable and Stable Species, Center for Molecular Science, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 100190 Beijing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Structural Chemistry of Unstable and Stable Species, Center for Molecular Science, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, 100049 Beijing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate School, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University,710075 Xi’an, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Engineering Research Center of Printed Electronics, Beijing Institute of Graphic Communication, 102600 Beijing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Engineering Research Center of Printed Electronics, Beijing Institute of Graphic Communication

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lysosome fluorescent imaging has been widely used in the field of biological staining and diagnostics, which plays a key role in understanding intracellular metabolism and various physiological processes. However, for most currently used small-molecule lysotrackers, the photostability is often unsatisfactory when used for long-term and real-time visualization of lysosomal dynamics. Herein, we reported a new lysosome-targetable photostable fluorescent probe (i.e. MPL-NPA), and results showed that MPL-NAP possesses superior photostability, appreciable tolerance to pH change, low cytotoxicity and high lysosome targeting ability. These findings confirm that MPL-NAP is a well-suited imaging agent for targeting lysosome and enables long-term and real-time monitor of lysosome morphological changes under physiological processes.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence imaging has attracted considerable attention owing to its high sensitivity, spatiotemporal resolution, low cost, non-invasiveness and the ability for long-term observation, etc. Because of these advantages, it has been widely utilized in the field of bioimaging and biosensors [1–3]. However, there is a bottleneck for the development of fluorescent probes, that is, the photostability of the probes is often unsatisfactory and can’t meet the requirements of rapid evolution of fluorescence imaging techniques, particularly in super-resolution imaging techniques and long-time live-cell imaging [4,5]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop photostable fluorescent probes.

Lysosomes are acidic organelles in eukaryotic cells that contain digestive enzymes functioning to recycle damaged organelles as well as digest nucleic acids, polysaccharides, fats and proteins [6–8]. Lysosomes play a key role in various physiological processes, such as endocytosis, autophagy, apoptosis and so on [9–11]. Meanwhile, increasing evidences have shown that lysosomes are also involved in various pathologies including inflammation, tumor formation and several neurodegenerative diseases [12,13]. Besides, lysosomes are of a highly dynamic nature, and they are constantly changing their morphology and spatial distribution. Therefore, long-term and real-time tracking of their movements and morphologic changes will not only help to understand their working status, but also provide new theranostic strategies for diagnosing, preventing and treating lysosomal dysfunction-related diseases [14], which calls for efficient fluorescent probes of high specificity and photostability but low disturbance to lysosomes [15].

Neutral red, acridine orange and the LysoTracker probes such as LysoTracker Red DND-99 and LysoTracker Green DND-26, as commercial fluorescent stains, have already been widely utilized as markers for lysosome [12]. However, those fluorescent probes can be quickly photobleached when a harsh laser beam is used as the excitation light sources [16,17]. Poor photostability limited their application in long-time and live-cell imaging. To overcome this deficiency, it is necessary to develop a light-stable lysosomal probe [18]. In recent years, several attempts have been reported. Zhang's group reported a boron-dipyrromethene-based lysosome tracker, Lyso-NIR, which can stably localize in lysosomes with high photostability [15]. Liu's group designed a lysosome-targeting AIE fluorescent probe tetraphenylethene-morpholine, TPE-MPL, by incorporating morpholine group into tetraphenylethene skeleton [12]. Chang's group reported a BODIPY-derived compound, CDy6, which can be used to achieve long-term and real-time visualization of lysosomal dynamics [19].

Although many photostable lysosomal probes have been reported, it is still necessary to further optimize the performance of probes in terms of specificity and photostability. Herein, we developed a new lysosomal photostable fluorescent probe, i.e. MPL-NAP, by incorporating a typical lysosome-targeting moiety of morpholine into a stable 1,8-naphthalimide skeleton [20]. Compared with the commercially available fluorescent probes, MPL-NAP can light up lysosomes specifically in live cells with superior photostability, and enable long-term and real-time visualization of lysosome morphological changes in the face of external environment change. Additionally, MPL-NAP shows excellent biocompatibility, and the synthesis and purification process is simple and low-cost. These advantages qualify it as a reliable tracker for lysosomes under physiological processes.

Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM), penicillin, fetal bovine serum (FBS), streptomycin, 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)− 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), LysoTracker Red DND-99, LysoTracker Green DND-26, Hoechst 33342, MitoTracker® Deep Red FM, Cytoplasm Tracker CMAC were purchased from Invitrogen Co. (Beijing, China). All solvents and chemicals were reagent grade and applied without further purification unless otherwise stated.

4-bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride, morpholine, 4-(2-aminoethyl) morpholine, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), silica gel (200–300 mesh) utilized for flash column chromatography and other reagents were all purchased from J&K Chemical. Ultrapure water (Milli-Q plus, Millipore Inc., Bedford, MA) was used throughout. Buffer concentrates pH 2.0–11.0 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and diluted to 500 ml for further use.

UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded in a conventional quartz cell (light path 10 mm) by using a UV-3310 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Confocal images were scanned by Laser Confocal Scanning Biological Microscope FV1000-IX81 (Olympus, Japan). The absorbance for MTT analysis was recorded on a microplate reader (BIO-TEK Synergy HT, USA) at 490 nm.

1H and 13C NMR spectra were measured on a Bruker Avance III 400 HD spectrometer in CDCl3 or DMSO-d 6. High-resolution mass spectra were recorded on Bruker Daltonics Inc. Autoflex III mass spectrometer.

The synthetic route to MPL–NAP is shown in Scheme 1. Firstly, compound 2 was synthesized according to the method described previously [21], that is, 4-bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride (compound 1) (1.1 g, 4 mmol) and morpholine (0.7 g, 8 mmol) were dissolved in 50 ml DMF and heated to 100 °C for 24 h. After cooling to RT, the solvent was removed under vacuum. The product was crystallized with methanol and a yellow powder was gotten (compound 2). Yield: 0.96 g, 85%. 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3, TMS): δ 8.60 (d, J = 7.2 Hz, 1 H), 8.54 (d, J = 8.1 Hz, 1 H), 8.48 (d, J = 8.5 Hz, 1 H), 7.76 (t, J = 7.5 Hz, 1 H), 7.26 (d, J = 8.0 Hz, 1 H), 4.44 (t, J = 4.5 Hz, 4 H), 4.04 (t, J = 4.1 Hz, 4 H).

Secondly, to a mixture of compound 2 (0.85 g, 3 mmol) in anhydrous ethanol was partly added 4-(2-aminoethyl) morpholine (1.56 g, 12 mmol). The mixture was stirred and heated to reflux for 10 h under nitrogen. After cooling to RT, the solvent was removed under vacuum. The product was crystallized with methanol and retained a yellow crystal MPL-NAP in 73% yield (0.87 g). 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3): δ 8.71–8.57 (m, 1 H), 8.57–8.49 (m, 1 H), 8.43 (dd, J = 8.5, 1.2 Hz, 1 H), 7.71 (dd, J = 8.4, 7.2 Hz, 1 H), 7.24 (d, J = 8.1 Hz, 1 H), 4.33 (t, J = 7.0 Hz, 2 H), 4.02 (t, J = 4.6 Hz, 4 H), 3.68 (t, J = 4.6 Hz, 4 H), 3.27 (dd, J = 5.7, 3.4 Hz, 4 H), 2.70 (t, J = 7.0 Hz, 2 H), 2.60 (t, J = 4.6 Hz, 4 H).13C NMR (400 MHz, DMSO-d 6, TMS): δ 164.40, 163.93, 155.64, 132.50, 131.16, 130.07, 129.92, 126.18, 125.85, 123.32, 114.98, 67.07, 66.97, 56.20, 53.82, 53.45, 37.11; Elemental analysis: C: 66.82%; H: 6.37%; N: 10.63%; O: 16.18%; HRMS (ESI-TOF) calculated for C22H26N3O4: 396.191933, found: m/z = 396.191783.

To evaluate the cytotoxicity of MPL-NAP, a standard MTT assay was carried out using three different cell lines (MCF-7, RAW264.7 and H9C2 cells). Cells were seeded at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well in 96-well U-bottom plates, and incubated with MPL-NAP at a concentration range from 0 to 5000 nM. Each concentration was tested in 6 wells. After 24 h, the culture media were discarded, the cells were washed with PBS (phosphate-buffered saline) buffer (pH 7.4), and 0.1 ml of MTT solution (0.5 mg/ml in DMEM) was added to each well. After incubation for another 4 h, the medium containing MTT was carefully removed from each well and 150 μl of dimethyl sulfoxide was added into each well. After shaking the plates for 10 min, absorbance values of the wells were recorded with a microplate reader at 490 nm. Each of the experiments was performed at least 3 times.

MCF-7, RAW264.7, H9C2 and SH-SY5Y cells were purchased from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC). The cells were cultured in DMEM medium with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum and 1% (v/v) penicillin-streptomycin at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator.

RAW264.7 and H9C2 cells were grown overnight on four confocal microscope dishes with a cover slip and 500 nM MPL-NPA was added to cells for 30 min, respectively. After washing with PBS buffer three times, the live cells were stained with 50 nM LysoTracker Red DND-99, 10 μg/μl Hoechst 33324, 50 nM MitoTracker® Deep Red FM and 10 μM Cytoplasm Tracker CMAC for cell imaging. After incubation for 15 min, the live cells were washed with PBS buffer three times. Then subcellular organelle selectivity analysis of MPL-NPA were imaged by confocal microscope.

MCF-7, RAW264.7, SH-SY5Y and H9C2 cells were grown overnight on a confocal microscope dish with a cover slip, respectively. 500 nM MPL-NAP was added to cells for 30 min. After washing with PBS buffer three times, the live cells were stained with 50 nM LysoTracker Red DND-99 for 15 min and washed with PBS buffer three times. Subcellular localization analysis of MPL-NPA and LysoTracker Red DND-99 was imaged by confocal microscope. MPL-NPA was excited at 488 nm and the fluorescence was collected at 490–555 nm. LysoTracker Red DND-99 was excited at 559 nm and fluorescence was collected at 580–700 nm.

Firstly, the photodegradation test was carried out in square cross-section quartz cells (1 × 1 cm) to evaluate fluorescence photostability of MPL-NPA. The solution of sample was continuously irradiated with a 400 W iodine-tungsten lamp at room temperature for 1 h and the fluorescence at 550 nm was collected.

Secondly, H9C2 cells cultured with MPL-NPA and LysoTracker probes were scanned by confocal microscope for a long term. MPL-NPA was excited at 488 nm and the fluorescence was collected at 490–555 nm. LysoTracker Red DND-99 was excited at 559 nm and fluorescence was collected at 580–700 nm, LysoTracker Green DND-26 was excited at 488 nm and fluorescence was collected at 500–555 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-time monitoring the dynamic variation of lysosome in cell apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: H9C2 cells were grown 24 h on a 35-mm petri dish with a coverslip. The live cells were stained with 500 nM MPL–NAP for 30 min. Then, 250 μmol/L H2O2 were added into the dish, and the cells were imaged by confocal microscopy with Delta Vision OMX SR High Resolution Live Cell Imaging System. The lysosome morphological changes in H9C2 cells were then observed during apoptosis.

The Caenorhabditis elegans wild type strain N2 was acquired from the Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. The larval stage 4 (L4) Caenorhabditis elegans was utilized to expose the L4 stage nematodes to MPL-NAP, three Petri dishes were first filled with 10 ml of M9 buffer (5.8 g Na2HPO4·7H2O, 5 g NaCl, 3 g KH2PO4 and 0.25 g MgSO4·7H2O dissolved in 1 L water) supplemented with 2 μM of MPL-NAP and 100 nM LysoTracker Red DND-99, respectively. These L4 stage worms were then incubated in the plates at 20 °C for 2 h. After incubation, the exposed nematodes were washed three times with M9 buffer by centrifugation at 3000 r/min for 2 min. Then, Caenorhabditis elegans was mounted onto a slide glass. The images of the mounted nematodes were acquired by Carl Zeiss. Observer Z1 Laser Scan Confocal Microscope.

Statistical analyses were performed using SPSS version 18.0 software (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL). Means were calculated from at least three independent experiments, and error bars represent standard error of the mean.

1,8-naphthalimide has a large family of derivatives which have been extensively employed in fluorescent cellular imaging fields because of their strong photostability and low cytotoxicity [22]. The extraordinarily high photostability can be attributed to their extremely stable structure owing to the high π-conjugated macrocycle [23,24]. In this work, to achieve stable imaging of lysosomes, morpholine group, a typical lysosome-targeting moiety was introduced to 1,8-naphthalimide, together with 4-(2-aminoethyl) morpholine [25,26]. MPL-NAP was synthesized according to the synthetic route shown in Scheme 1. The product was obtained as a yellow crystal in a yield of 62% and characterized by high-resolution mass spectroscopy and NMR. The detailed synthetic route is described in Experimental Section and detailed characterization data are accessible in the ESI (Fig. S1-S4).

MPL-NAP was initially evaluated for its spectroscopic properties ( Fig. 1) under near physiological conditions (PBS buffer, pH = 7.4). As shown in Fig. 1a and b, the probe itself demonstrated the maximum ultraviolet absorption peak at 406 nm and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were shifted to 462 nm and 550 nm, respectively. As expected, the Stokes shift of MPL-NAP was 144 nm, which is approximately 5 times longer than that of BODIPY-based probes (Table S1) and large Stokes shift helps to reduce self-absorption and the measuring error. Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield of MPL-NAP was 5.3% in PBS buffer containing 0.5% CTAB (Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium Bromide), which is a little lower than that of commercial lysosomal probe (Table S2), for example LysoTracker Red DND-99 [27].

Most currently used lysosomal tracker probes are pH sensitive and their targeting-ability is based on the alkalization effect [28,29]. The pH response of MPL-NAP was also thoroughly examined. As shown in Fig. 1c, the fluorescence intensity of MPL-NAP was insensitive to pH ranging from 2.0 to 11.0. The results indicated that MPL-NAP imaging was not influenced by pH of the microenvironment. Such stable fluorescence intensity independent of pH change is important for correctly indicating the location and morphology of lysosomes in vivo.

Firstly, the specific selectivity of MPL-NPA to subcellular organelles was tested by co-staining of H9C2 cells with four commercially representative fluorescent organelle trackers (MitoTracker® Deep Red FM for mitochondria, Hoechst 33324 for cell nucleus and LysoTracker Red DND-99 for lysosomes, CMAC for cytoplasm). The fluorescent co-localization images of MPL-NAP with those known representative trackers ( Fig. 2) indicated that MPL-NAP has a high co-registration effect with LysoTracker Red DND-99 (Fig. 2a). The yellow staining in the merged image was generated by the superposition of green MPL-NAP and red LysoTracker Red DND-99. However, poor co-stained effect was observed between MPL-NAP with Hoechst 33324, MitoTracker® Deep Red FM and Cytoplasm Tracker CMAC (Fig. 2b-d), demonstrating that MPL-NAP could not mark the cell nucleus or mitochondria but selectively label lysosomes. We also used RAW264.7 cells to test the specific selectivity of MPL-NPA to subcellular organelles, consistent results were shown in Fig. S5.

Secondly, to further examine its universal lysosomes-selective staining capability, MPL-NAP was evaluated in additional three cell lines, MCF-7, RAW264.7, and SH-SY5Y cells. As shown in Fig. 3, confocal fluorescence images and colocalization results with LysoTracker Red DND-99 further confirmed that the observed green fluorescence from MPL-NAP is specially localized to the lysosomes of the living cells. Similar co-localization effect can be found in both cancer cells and non-cancerous cells (Fig. 3a-d). What's more, confocal fluorescence images in Z axis can scan cell in various depth (Fig. 3e), the result indicated that MPL-NAP deeply permeated into cellular lysosomes. All these results consistently demonstrated that MPL-NAP is a potential universal lysosomal tracker for various live cells.

Moreover, a magnified image from RAW264.7 cells was shown in Fig. 4a-d, the intensity profiles of the linear regions of interest across RAW264.7 cells stained with MPL-NAP and LysoTracker Red DND-99 also varied in close synchrony (Fig. 4e). From the intensity correlation plots (Fig. 4f), we can find that fluorescent signals of the two dyes from different channels are perfectly overlapped and a high Pearson's coefficient and overlap coefficient of 0.915 and 1.616, respectively, were obtained. The above colocalization investigation confirmed that MPL-NAP can stain lysosomes specifically.

Photobleaching is an unavoidable problem for most organic dyes that will compromise the temporal monitoring of dynamic events inside cells [30,31]. Many existing fluorophores lack sufficient photostability and can’t meet the requirements of rapid evolution of fluorescence imaging techniques, particularly in super-resolution imaging techniques and long-time live-cell imaging. Here, photodecomposition experiments proved that MPL-NAP has better photostability compared with commercial lysosomal tracker probes in living cells. As shown in Fig. S6, after MPL-NAP in PBS buffer was continuously irradiated with a 400 W iodine tungsten lamp at room temperature for about 1 h, more than 95% of the fluorescence intensity of MPL-NAP remained compared to its original fluorescence, indicating that MPL-NPA has a satisfactory photostability.

To further evaluate whether MPL-NAP is also photostable for lysosomal imaging in living cells, H9C2 cells labeled with LysoTracker Red DND-99 were separately co-stained with MPL-NAP and LysoTracker Green DND-26, then they were continuously scanned 30 times by confocal microscopy [32]. The fluorescent properties of three probes were measured after laser irradiation respectively ( Fig. 5 and S7). Fig. 5a-c show that the fluorescent intensity of LysoTracker Red DND-99 decreased more obviously than that of MPL-NAP. Moreover, Fig. 5d reveals the fluorescence intensity decay of MPL-NAP compared with LysoTracker Red DND-99 and LysoTracker Green DND-26, and results showed that the fluorescence signals of LysoTracker Red DND-99 almost disappeared after 30 scans with about 13% signal intensity remaining, while the fluorescence signals of LysoTracker Green DND-26 remained 32%. In contrast, the signal loss of MPL-NAP was less than 15% during 30 scans with an entire irradiation time of 21 min (Videos S1–S3), and no notable difference was observed between the first and the last scan. Based on these results we concluded that MPL-NAP has an excellent photostability property than commercial lysosomal tracker probes and it is well suitable for long-term and real-time lysosomal tracking.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.06.001.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video 1, Video 2, Video 3.
Video 1
Confocal microscopy images of H9C2 cells stained with MPL-NAP (0.5 μM) with an increasing number of scans (1–30 scans).
Video 2
Confocal microscopy images of H9C2 cells stained with LysoTracker Red DND-99 with an increasing number of scans (1–30 scans).
Video 3
The merged image of H9C2 cells co-stained with MPL-NAP and LysoTracker Red DND-99 with an increasing number of scans (1–30 scans).

Since low cell toxicity is a key feature for live-cell imaging [33], the cytotoxicity of MPL-NAP was determined by MTT assay in three kinds of cells: MCF-7, H9C2 and RAW264.7. Fig. 6 reveals that even incubated within a much higher concentration (5 μM) than the optimized level for staining, the cell survival rates were above 90% after being incubated for 24 h. These results suggested that MPL-NAP exhibited a low cytotoxicity against cells even under high concentration and long-time incubation. It is suitable for long-time physiological processes and can be further used in biological system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-time monitoring the dynamic variation of lysosome in cell apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: As a tightly regulated process of programmed cell death, apoptosis has been improper regulation in various cancers, autoimmune diseases and neurodegenerative disorders [34,35]. Since MPL-NAP could be stably localized in lysosome and maintain stable fluorescence, its tracking performance was further utilized to monitor the dynamic variation of lysosome in cell apoptosis. Fluorescence signal changes located in lysosome in H9C2 cells were recorded and compared ( Fig. 7). As shown in Fig. 7a-d and Video S4, with the prolongation of stimulation time of H2O2 [36], the level of apoptosis gradually increased in a time-dependent manner [37–39]. In contrast, lysosomal fluorescence signal of MPL-NAP decreased significantly. It was reported that the cytosol was acidified during apoptosis [40], which might lead to the pH change of the lysosome [41–43], then resulting in the escape of the probe from lysosomes, and thus weaken the fluorescence intensity. Based on these results it can be concluded that MPL-NAP can act as an indicator to monitor the dynamic variation of lysosome in cell apoptosis. The successful long-term and real-time imaging of MPL-NAP in living cells indicated its utility as a superior lysosome tracker in cell biology.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.06.001.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video 4.
Video 4
Long-time and live-cells confocal images of H9C2 cells stained with 0.5 μM MPL-NAP and stimulated using 250 μM H2O2.

We examined the application potential of MPL-NAP for lysosomal imaging in Caenorhabditis elegans [44]. As shown in Fig. 8, the green fluorescence of MPL-NAP can effectively cover the red region of LysoTracker Red DND-99 (Fig. 8a-d). Meanwhile, the granular lysosomes can be clearly observed by these two dyes under confocal microscopy (Fig. 8e-f). Different signals from two channels were highly overlapped with a high Pearson's coefficient (0.94), confirming that MPL-NAP can also be utilized to localize lysosomes of Caenorhabditis elegans. These results indicate that MPL-NAP has a great potential for lysosomal imaging in vivo.

In this study, we successfully designed and synthesized a photostable lysosomal targeting fluorescence probe, MPL-NAP, for long-term and real-time visualization of the dynamic variation of lysosomes. MPL-NAP can monitor the lysosomes without tedious sample preparation or expensive reagent consumption. On the one hand, MPL-NAP exhibits low cytotoxicity and good stability under various conditions. On the other hand, it shows satisfactory specificity for lysosomes and can clearly distinguish lysosomes from other organelles in living cells, based on the morpholine moiety which gives the high specificity to lysosomes. As the most important performance, in comparison with the commercial lysosomal fluorescence probes, MPL-NAP shows better photostability under continuous UV illumination and laser irradiation in living cells and provides stable and reliable fluorescence, thus resulting in the long-term measurement ability. Overall, MPL-NAP offers a highly convenient, high specificity, low cytotoxicity and long-term and real-time method for monitoring lysosomes in living cells. It may provide a more stable testing platform for cell imaging, and has a very optimistic prospect in the process of biological signal observations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Sciences Foundation of China (No. 31571020, 31570856 and 31300697) and a cross training program for high level talents of Beijing universities (No. 03150117009/002/010).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest. The authors alone are responsible for the content and writing of the paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.06.001.
